retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Palate -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Furthermore, while CL/P and CPO frequently occur in isolation, in up to 30% of cases, these defects can also be part of a congenital syndrome, which must be recognized to initiate early interprofessional management. [1] This resource primarily discusses an overview of cleft palate. Please see StatPearls' companion resources, " Cleft Palate Repair ," " Cleft Lip ," and " Cleft Lip Repair ," for more detailed information.

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Palate -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: CL/P is the most common craniofacial defect in the newborn and the fourth most common overall congenital anomaly. The literature quotes a prevalence of anywhere from 1 per 650 to 1000 live births; Asian patients are affected twice as commonly as White patients. Males are 2 times more commonly affected than females.

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Palate -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Cleft lip and palate, one of the most common congenital craniofacial abnormalities, is characterized by failure of normal fusion of the palate and lip during development, resulting in a clinically evident discontinuity of the lip or palate at birth. Cleft lip and palate (CL/P) and cleft palate alone (CPO) are not only cosmetic deformities but have significant functional morbidity for the newborn without adequate management. CL/P impacts the newborn's ability to feed in multiple ways, including increased nasal reflux, inability to form an adequate latch, and increased work of feeding, leading to fatigue.

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Lip -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Cleft lip usually develops at the junction between the lateral and central segments of the upper lip. The cleft usually affects the upper lip and may extend into the maxilla and palate.

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Palate Repair -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: During prenatal consultations, a common parental concern is a risk of having a child with a cleft lip and/or cleft palate. For parents with no history of cleft lip and palate and who have had one child with a cleft lip and palate, the chances of having a second child with a cleft are approximately 4%. When parents have had two children with cleft lip and palate, the likelihood of having a third increase to about 9%. If one parent has had a cleft lip and palate and one sibling has a cleft, the risk rises to approximately 17%. Recurrence risk increases in cases of more severe clefts.

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Palate -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: While some rare environmental factors are associated with cleft palate, the majority are spontaneous. A familial risk of approximately 2%, including spontaneous cases, of a parent with a cleft having a child with a cleft has been reported, even in the absence of a genetic syndrome. [69] Genetic counseling should therefore be offered to every family affected by CL/P, particularly for patients aging out of interprofessional teams that may be considering starting their own families. Genetic counseling is particularly significant in those patients and families with identified genetic syndromes.

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Palate Repair -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Management of palatal clefting requires an appreciation of the anatomy and embryology as well as the physiology of the upper aerodigestive tract and how it relates to speech, swallowing, breathing, and hearing. The incidence of palatal clefting ranges from 1 to 25 per 10,000 live births, with the exact number primarily dependent on patient ethnicity; White-race individuals are most likely to be affected, and Blacks are least likely. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Palate Repair -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Cleft palates come in many forms, including submucous clefting, secondary palate clefting, primary and secondary palate clefting, and complete clefting, which includes the alveolar ridge and lip. Cleft width, laterality, and degree of velopharyngeal dysfunction vary among individuals, as do comorbidities. Roughly half of all palatal clefting cases are nonsyndromic, but myriad syndromes may also be associated with cleft palates. Every patient is different, and every cleft palate is potentially unique. [2] For this reason, a thorough understanding of the treatment options as well as the potential sequelae of both palatal clefting and cleft palate repair, are essential for the interprofessional healthcare team to treat these patients safely, optimizing outcomes and minimizing complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Palate -- Etiology -- Molecular and Environmental Factors Associated with Cleft Palet
retrieved_docs.content: Significant heterogeneity exists in the molecular pathways associated with the development of CL/P and CPO. The pathways associated with the formation of the lip and palate include the sonic hedgehog pathway ( SHH and SPRY2 genes), which interacts closely with the bone morphogenic pathway ( BMP4 and BMP2 genes), fibroblast growth factor pathways ( FGF10 and FGF7 genes), and transforming growth factor-beta (TGFβ) receptors and ligands. Disruption of these pathways at various stages results in varying severity, laterality, and location of clefts. [9] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Palate -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: A global study by the National Institute for Dental and Craniofacial Research, including over 7.5 million births, found a prevalence of CL/P of 6.64 per 10,000 births, including stillbirths and terminations. For cleft palate alone, the prevalence was 3.28 per 10,000. Of these, 77% were isolated, 16% had other malformations, and 7% had a recognized syndrome. [7] [10] [12] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Palate -- Complications -- Complications Due to an Unrepaired Cleft Palate
retrieved_docs.content: The optimal time for intervention of 11 to 12 months appears to the most effective for minimizing facial growth disturbances and long-term speech development issues. Likewise, the use of McGovern nipples on bottles, or breastfeeding, seeks to maximize feeding and nutrition, and the need for a nasogastric tube or other alternate alimentary feeding routes is uncommon in the absence of genetic syndromes. [67] The goal is to repair the palate and allow the child to become accustomed to using the repaired anatomy in the prelingual years. This approach maximizes speech, growth, nutritional, and development outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Palate -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Prenatal magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is useful for further characterization of the cleft and is used when a CL/P has already been identified to evaluate for other defects, especially if a potential for additional intracranial findings in the setting of a known or suspected genetic syndrome is present. Prenatal identification allows for parental counseling and earlier intervention. [10] [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Palate -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: In the immediate postpartum period, neonates are obligate nasal breathers. The abnormal communication between the mouth and nose can lead to the tongue protruding into the nasal cavity and interrupting normal nasal breathing. In addition to hampering the ability of the infant to latch onto a nipple, a cleft palate can interfere with primary feeding and the suck-swallow-breathe coordination necessary for maintaining nutrition. [15] Long-term effects can also include dental malposition, abnormal facial growth, and catastrophic psychosocial effects due to stigmata associated with cleft speech and nasal regurgitation of food. [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Palate Repair -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Cleft palate and cleft lip with cleft palate are commonly-encountered conditions with multiple etiologies. Cleft palate repair aims to reconstruct the palatal muscle sling, provide an anatomical foundation for adequate palatal movement and good speech, isolate the oral cavity from the nasal cavity, and repair the palatal defect. Cleft width, laterality, and degree of velopharyngeal dysfunction vary among individuals, as do comorbidities. Roughly half of all palatal clefting cases are nonsyndromic, but many other syndromes may also be associated with cleft palates. This activity details some of the operative techniques of cleft palate repair and highlights the interprofessional healthcare team's role in evaluating and treating patients with cleft palates.

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Palate Repair -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Evidence-based guidelines regarding the best approach for cleft palate repair are not available because of the heterogeneity of cleft palate types, heterogeneity of technique, heterogeneity of follow-up (because patients with palatal clefting may experience lifelong sequelae despite early surgical management), and patient characteristics (non-syndromic vs. syndromic, etc.)

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Lip -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: In males, cleft lips most commonly occur on the left side. There is a 0.1% overall risk of cleft lip with or without cleft palate in the general population. In the Asian population, cleft lip is more common, with 2 out of 1000 babies born with cleft lip versus 1 out of 1000 in Caucasians and 0.5 out of 1000 in African Americans. Twenty-nine percent of children with cleft lip have associated congenital malformations. Cleft lip formation is most likely influenced by a patient’s genetic make-up but is multi-factorial. An expectant mother's malnourishment as well as exposure to phenytoin, steroids, tobacco, alcohol, and Accutane is known to increase the likelihood of cleft lip deformity. Folate, however, has been found to be preventative for cleft lip formation. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: The mentum presentation of the fetus, in which the fetal chin is the leading part during delivery, is classified as a مّجيء وَجهي (face presentation). Recognizing this presentation is important for obstetric assessment and delivery planning, as it influences the choice of birth method and the management of potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1226
retrieved_docs.content: these specific presentations, creating a hierarchical structure that integrates Face or brow presentation, Prolapsed arm of fetus, Transverse or oblique presentation, and Unspecified malposition or malpresentation under a unified framework. This integrated representation supports clinicians in identifying, categorizing, and managing various abnormal fetal presentations to optimize maternal‑fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 277
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are associated with nasal disorder (اِضطِراب أنفي).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2873
retrieved_docs.content: The condition referred to as اضطراب جنيني (fetal disorder) is classified as a type of مضاعفات الحمل (pregnancy complication). Recognizing that fetal disorders are encompassed within the broader category of pregnancy complications helps clinicians identify, monitor, and manage these issues during prenatal care, ensuring appropriate assessment and intervention for both maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 850
retrieved_docs.content: care during pregnancy, labor, and delivery. This integrated framework supports comprehensive obstetric assessment by recognizing how fetal presentation anomalies and maternal surgical history jointly inform the need for specialized care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2044
retrieved_docs.content: Face OR brow presentation of fetus is a specific fetal presentation that has as its parent the broader category Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable. This broader category is classified as a Malposition; malpresentation, indicating that the fetus is positioned abnormally in the birth canal. Understanding that both Face OR brow presentation of fetus and the unspecified Face or brow presentation fall under the umbrella of malposition or malpresentation informs clinicians that these presentations may complicate labor and delivery, prompting careful obstetric assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 243
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of teeth and jaw are classified under the broader parent category zuby and are specifically associated with the anatomical location of zuby mléčné, the primary (milk) teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1843
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation are classified under the term تقييد النمو الجنيني, which denotes fetal growth restriction. This condition is understood to be part of a broader, unspecified disorder that originates in the perinatal period, referred to as حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة. Recognizing that تقييد النمو الجنيني falls within this larger perinatal context helps clinicians assess and manage infants presenting with these growth‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 944
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal growth restriction (تقييد النمو الجنيني) is described as having a broader relationship with an unspecified condition that originated in the perinatal period (حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة), indicating that fetal growth restriction can be viewed as a more general clinical category under which certain undefined perinatal conditions may be classified, underscoring the importance of thorough assessment of growth abnormalities around the time of birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 259
retrieved_docs.content: and to the neurosurgical and spinal explorations. Additional Excision applications include therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth and pharynx and are related to plastic procedures on the nose. Together, Exploration provides an umbrella framework for targeted surgical investigations, while Excision represents the core operative technique that enables tissue removal, obstruction relief, diagnostic assessment and neural decompression, thereby optimizing patient outcomes across multiple specialties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1075
retrieved_docs.content: Face OR brow presentation of fetus is a specific fetal presentation that is linked hierarchically to the more general term “Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” indicating that the former is a child concept of the latter. This generic face‑or‑brow presentation is classified as a type of malposition; malpresentation, placing it within the broader category of abnormal fetal positions. Similarly, Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable, and Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus are each connected to their respective generic descriptors (“Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” and “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable”) through a “has parent” relationship, showing that the specific fetal findings are sub‑categories of the unnamed generic forms. Both the prolapsed arm and the transverse/oblique presentations are also

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: and cardiovascular complications are a subset of the overall perinatal condition spectrum. Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth trajectories define a network of classifications—from specific gestational milestones (29‑30 weeks) to broad perinatal disorder groups—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding, diagnosing, and managing health issues in liveborn infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 958
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Malposition; malpresentation” serves as a hierarchical classification for several abnormal fetal presentations. Within this group, “Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” is classified as a malposition/malpresentation and functions as the parent term for the specific entity “Face OR brow presentation of fetus.” Likewise, “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” is also classified as a malposition/malpresentation and acts as the parent term for the specific entity “Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus.” In addition to these presentations, the malposition/malpresentation category includes “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable.” Together, these relationships delineate a structured taxonomy of fetal malpositions that is essential for obstetric diagnosis, risk assessment, and the planning of appropriate intrapartum management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 189
retrieved_docs.content: each condition as a potential adverse event, enabling systematic risk assessment, targeted monitoring, operative decision‑making, and coordinated maternal‑fetal and newborn care aimed at reducing morbidity and improving outcomes for both mother and infant.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 109
retrieved_docs.content: disorders, and obstetric interventions together form a comprehensive framework for risk assessment, care planning, and management of maternal‑fetal outcomes during pregnancy, delivery, and the postpartum period.
